title: Let Us Continue Our Journey of Self-Respect without Falling Prey to Intrigue!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. The six source pages were checked for the 2012 letter quotation, media headlines, personal names, Periyar's speech, and page joins. A repeated title fragment and visually clear OCR character and spacing errors were corrected only in this translation file.
paragraphs: On 23 October 2012, I wrote a letter to my brethren in Murasoli. Yesterday, I spent the entire day rereading that letter, titled “Can Water Be Separated by Striking Water?” I did so because the need had arisen for me to read it again.

I sat down for lunch. Our Kazhagam Principal Secretary, younger brother Duraimurugan, came to see me after completing his work in the Legislative Assembly. His face was troubled. As I ate, I asked what had happened. He said: “The Times of India has carried a front-page report about an interview given by T.K.S. Elangovan. Journalists are waiting to ask questions about it. At the Secretariat, reporters asked our Kazhagam Treasurer, younger brother M.K. Stalin, who is in the Assembly, about the matter. He sent me to consult the Leader.”

I immediately called our Kazhagam Organisation Secretary, younger brother R.S. Bharathi, and asked him to issue a statement on behalf of headquarters. Then I lay down. Sleep would not come. I had intended to attend a wedding reception that evening, but I did not go. Why was my mind so disturbed? What caused it? It was in that state that I took out and reread the letter I had written in 2012. Some passages from it follow:

“I have often said one thing, always noting that Thanthai Periyar used to stress it. Among the Kazhagam's guiding watchwords, Duty, Dignity, and Discipline, it is Discipline that matters most! Our esteemed Professor recalled and explained this at length at the Mupperum Vizha in Villupuram.

“The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam may have lost many elections and won many elections. At no time have we run riot over victory or imagined ourselves destroyed by defeat. But if discipline breaks down or is damaged, the Kazhagam cannot afterwards be saved.

“The Professor and I have laboured without rest from the days when we were both students until now. If we chose to rest and stopped coming to the Kazhagam office, there would be no one to question us. Yet we continue to come. The Kazhagam is not now the ruling party. In the 2011 Tamil Nadu election, we suffered a defeat so severe that we could not even become the principal Opposition. Did I collapse because of it? Did I stop doing Kazhagam work?

“Why do I write every day even at this age? Whether or not I eat in the morning, how many newspapers do I read? You cannot buy and read them all. So I read every one of them, search out the reports you must know and explain to the people, and tirelessly compile for you the news and criticism appearing about the Government, along with the observations and condemnations expressed by the Supreme Court and High Court about this regime.

“Yet newspapers in Tamil Nadu publish my views grudgingly, two or three paragraphs at most, merely to create the appearance that they, too, have carried them and are neutral newspapers. More than even the rulers, our journalists seem anxious that publishing those views in full and allowing them to spread might increase the Kazhagam's influence. At the same time, when news hostile to the Kazhagam appears, they give it immense importance, print it without omitting a line, embrace the rulers, and come forward to assist them politically. Why? More than hatred towards us, it is their determination to protect the rulers.”
The condition I described in 2012 continues today. Even if the newspapers find a piece of news against us no bigger than a splinter, they enlarge it into a pillar.

T.K.S. Elangovan is the headquarters spokesperson. He is the beloved son of Thanjai T.K. Srinivasan, whom we praised as a “philosophical scholar.” He is well educated. But he has a particular enthusiasm for press reports and interviews. That is why, in accordance with his own interest, we appointed him Headquarters Communications Secretary after he had served as Organisation Secretary. Yet everything has a limit, does it not? Should that limit not be respected?

The Times of India itself must have recognised that he had crossed the line; otherwise, why did it carry his interview so prominently on both its first and eighth pages? Matters that should be released only after headquarters convenes the Executive Committee or General Council and holds a discussion were presented in that interview as ordinary remarks, whether or not T.K.S. Elangovan actually said them, and the English newspaper magnified them.

Was that not enough for our media? They surrounded every senior Kazhagam leader. His interview appeared on television and in Maalai Malar. By yesterday afternoon, we had issued a statement saying that the interview was wrong and had nothing to do with headquarters. Yet do you know the banner headline on the front page of this morning's Dinamalar? “Conflict Erupts in DMK over Alliance Negotiations! Karunanidhi Angered by Interview from Stalin Supporter.” It also published Elangovan's photograph.

In truth, it was younger brother Stalin who brought T.K.S. Elangovan's interview to my attention. But Dinamalar headlines the story, “Karunanidhi Angered by Interview from Stalin Supporter.” Can we not understand from that the depth of its malice towards us?

It is the same with what some publish under the name of an “opinion poll.” It produces no benefit, and I do not believe it. In truth, they publish such polls as though purchasing a quarrel at a price. A proper poll would ask whether the Chief Minister's office would go to the DMK, AIADMK, or DMDK. Instead, when asking who should be Chief Minister, they list Jayalalithaa for the AIADMK, but rather than listing the DMK, they ask how many support Karunanidhi and how many support Stalin. Why name two people from one party in a way designed to manufacture a problem?

Did they ask how many in the AIADMK support Jayalalithaa and how many support O. Panneerselvam? Why measure two individuals only in the DMK? Was the objective of those who conducted and published the poll to create confusion within the Kazhagam? Why are they attempting to create such confusion even before the election draws near?

When we ourselves are not worried about who will be Chief Minister from the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, why are the pollsters so concerned? Even after younger brother Stalin has repeatedly said that Kazhagam Leader Kalaignar will become Chief Minister for a sixth time, why are they so keen to prolong the dispute? What embarrassment this creates for Stalin! He labours night and day without fatigue, saying that the Kazhagam is his very breath. Why do they cause him such anguish?

Some journalists, displaying still greater eagerness, find out when the flight carrying my son M.K. Alagiri, who has been expelled from the Kazhagam, will arrive in Chennai, go all the way to the airport, and ask him about this opinion poll. Why this sudden concern? When some newspapers give that interview special importance, is it because they care for him? Because he is angry with his younger brother, he praises me. What benefit comes from that? It gives our opponents an opening. It gives room to those who seek to come between the brothers and create confusion in the Kazhagam.
When Dinamalar prints both T.K.S. Elangovan's interview and M.K. Alagiri's interview in banner type on its front page, can you not understand the extent of its hostility towards us?

Once, at a public meeting in Tiruchi held after the death of Arignar Anna, Thanthai Periyar spoke. He said: “Karunanidhi and the members of that party often say, because Annadurai said so, that we must preserve Duty, Dignity, and Discipline. I say this not only to Karunanidhi, but to every party worker and comrade under Karunanidhi's leadership. Even if you forget duty, forget it. Even if you fail to uphold dignity, let it go. But discipline, never abandon that alone. That is what I tell the party comrades, my DMK friends.” Periyar stated it firmly and emphatically.

For if discipline exists, everyone together may even act without dignity. That was Periyar's interpretation. Duty, too, may be neglected. But if discipline remains, no one can do anything to us. It is because we have followed to this day those words spoken by Periyar with such uncompromising force that no one has been able to shake or dislodge our Kazhagam. Today, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam stands like a banyan tree rising towards the sky.

But as though the rulers' efforts to obstruct its growth were not enough, a few newspapers operating in Tamil Nadu have been attempting all manner of things in support of them. Having dug a pit and buried the very principle of journalistic ethics, those newspapers engage in such intrigues. Should we ourselves become their prey? They do all this to divert the attention of Kazhagam activists. This letter is written so that Kazhagam members will not, and must not, fall prey to such efforts.

We conduct this incomparable movement even at the cost of tormenting ourselves, to preserve the name of our people, “Dravidian,” to safeguard ethnic consciousness, and to labour to spread it. Whatever intrigues our enemies employ to bring it down, we regard them all as “tiny waves that crash and fall against a mountain.” We have continued our journey of self-respect, and we shall continue it still!
With affection,
M.K.
5 September 2015
